coredata.dc:title: Red/NIR C-dots: A perspective from carbon precursors, photoluminescence tuning and bioapplications
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yuxiang
Zhao, Tianshu
Xu, Wenjun
Peng, Zhili
coredata.dc:description: Red/NIR carbon dots (C-dots) have obtained enormous interest in last decade thanks to their significant potentials in the biomedical fields. In this article, recent advancements on the synthesis approaches and carbon precursors for red/NIR C-dots preparation are comprehensively elaborated. Then reports on the fluorescence mechanism and emission manipulation of red/NIR C-dots are carefully discussed and highlighted. Furthermore, the recent progress of red/NIR C-dots for their biomedical applications are also closely surveyed. Last, perspective on the future trends of red/NIR C-dots is carefully proposed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red/NIR carbon dots (C-dots) have obtained enormous interest in last decade thanks to their significant potentials in the biomedical fields. In this article, recent advancements on the synthesis approaches and carbon precursors for red/NIR C-dots preparation are comprehensively elaborated. Then reports on the fluorescence mechanism and emission manipulation of red/NIR C-dots are carefully discussed and highlighted. Furthermore, the recent progress of red/NIR C-dots for their biomedical applications are also closely surveyed. Last, perspective on the future trends of red/NIR C-dots is carefully proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is among the most interested elements by material scientists and its capability to form diverse bonds within carbon atoms or with other elements lead to the born of a wide variety of carbon materials. Current carbon materials could be divided into three-, two-, one-, and zero-dimensional materials according to their sizes of three dimensions in space. Common three-dimensional materials include graphite and diamond; two-dimensional materials cover the most classic materials such as graphene, graphene oxide and similar ones; one-dimensional materials comprise carbon nanowires, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), etc.; zero-dimensional materials consist of carbon nanoparticles, nanodiamonds, fullerene and carbon dots (C-dots). Among these carbon materials, C-dots have attracted significant interests from researchers all over the world thanks to their superior properties such as excellent photoluminescence (PL), easy access, rich surface functionalities, facile modification and high biocompatibility [1–3]. Historically, C-dots were firstly discovered during the purification of oxidized single-walled CNTs in 2004 [4] and attained widely attention due to their superior tunable fluorescence emissions in 2006 [5]. In general, the family of C-dots could be classified as carbon nano dots (CNDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as well as carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) collectively based on their core structures, PL behaviors and carbon precursors (Fig. 1 ) [6]. CNDs generally possess a spherical or quasi-spherical amorphous carbon nucleus and CQDs have an obvious crystal lattice. GQDs are similar to CQDs in many aspects including presence of a crystal core and quantum confinement effect. Their differences arise from that GQDs are mainly derived from graphene-related materials with a layered carbon nucleus while CQDs could be derived from precursors of any origin and normally have spherical carbon cores. CPDs are formed by incomplete carbonization of C-dots, which could evolve into CQDs or CNDs through additional carbonization [7].

Owning to their novel properties, C-dots have been applied in a wide range of fields including catalysis, sensing and especially biomedicine. As the latest member of the carbon-based nanomaterials family, C-dots could participate in body metabolism with excellent luminescence performance and stability, thus are promising candidates as bioimaging agents. In addition, the easy surface modifications of C-dots render them the ability to load specific ligand or drug molecules to construct an integrated platform for diagnosis and treatment. Unfortunately, the value of C-dots emitting in the blue to green region is significantly discounted due to the severe interference by the strong autofluorescence of bio-samples. Furthermore, the ultraviolet (UV) light used to excite these C-dots might cause photic damage to tissues and stimulate cell mutation. By contrast, red/near-infrared (NIR) lights, falling between 600 and 1700 nm, have strong tissue penetrability and are less affected by the autofluorescence of bio-samples [8].

Hence, enormous efforts have been devoted to the syntheses and bioapplications of red/NIR C-dots in recent years. Back in 2007, Mao's group prepared C-dots that achieved emission at 615 nm [9], a few years later, Ge et al. succeeded the synthesis of deep red C-dots (680 nm) [10], which greatly advanced the field. Inspired by these pioneering studies, great advancements have been achieved for the synthesis and application of red/NIR C-dots [11,12]. As a result, several excellent review articles have been written to cover this rapidly developing field, which have greatly benefited researchers of the field. Still, majority of the articles are either outdated [13–16] or much attention has been focused on the frequently discussed aspects such as synthesis, mechanism, and applications [17–20]. The intriguing phenomena that certain intrinsic characteristics of carbon precursors could be inherited by the newly synthesized C-dots have attracted enormous attention and provided material scientists powerful tools to design and prepare red/NIR C-dots of desired properties [21–23]. Thus, a systematic review article not only updates the recent advancements on the syntheses, mechanism and bioapplications of red/NIR C-dots, but also analyzes the enormous progress specifically from the perspective of carbon precursors and photoluminescence tuning would be desired.

In this review article, the current methods for red/NIR C-dots syntheses including “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches are briefly summarized first. Then the key achievements on the preparation of red/NIR C-dots are comprehensively compiled and analyzed from the perspective of their carbon precursors, including citric acid, amines, phenols, polymers, cyanine dyes, biomass, biomolecules and inorganic chemicals. On the basis of the brief introduction of the four mainstream PL mechanisms (core-state, surface-state, molecular-state and CEE emissions), three fluorescence-engineering strategies (heteroatom doping, reaction parameters tuning and C-dots structure design) to guide the PL tuning of red/NIR C-dots are carefully discussed. Then, recent achievements of red/NIR C-dots for bioimaging, drug delivery, photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, multifunctional therapy as well as biosensing are also carefully updated. Lastly, future perspectives for the further advancements of red/NIR C-dots are cautiously proposed.

Similar to the preparation of general C-dots, the numerous methods for the synthesis of red/NIR C-dots could also be broadly divided as “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches (Fig. 2 A). In top-down synthesis, C-dots are prepared via the cracking of bulk carbon precursors (i.e., carbon powders, graphite, graphene, carbon nano tubes, etc.). Although the size of C-dots synthesized through “top down” strategy are controllable by tuning the “cracking force” applied [24,25], there are still some deficiencies such as demanding reaction conditions, limited carbon sources and low yields. In addition, the optical properties of “top-down” C-dots are generally inferior, which require separate surface modifications to enhance [26]. Thus, the adoption of “top-down” approach for the preparation of red/NIR C-dots was less reported [9].

On the other hand, red/NIR C-dots are generally prepared from small organic precursors via a “polymerization-carbonization” process in “bottom-up” approach [27]. Compared to “top-down” approach, the carbon precursors for “bottom-up” method are much more accessible and versatile, and the reaction conditions are less stringent. Moreover, the synthesis and surface modification or passivation of C-dots could be achieved in a “one-pot” fashion with high yield. Thus, “bottom-up” method has become the main approach for the preparation of NIR C-dots. In this context, the main focus of this section will be placed on the “bottom-up” synthesis of red/NIR C-dots.

Hydrothermal treatment is among the mostly applied synthesis methods for the preparation of red/NIR C-dots. By confining the aqueous solutions of the carbon precursors in a sealed container, the reaction could provide a perfect environment of high temperature and pressure to promote the “polymerization-carbonization” process for red/NIR C-dots formation. Depending on whether an extra step of doping or surface passivation is presented, hydrothermal synthesis could be classified as one-pot or stepwise preparation (Fig. 2B). Currently, most of hydrothermal reactions for red/NIR C-dots synthesis is carried out in a one-pot manner, in which C-dots formation and doping/surface passivation occur simultaneously [28–32]. Thanks to the facile synthesis, various doping agents (i.e., Ln, Se) could be introduced to the reaction to enhance the optical properties of C-dots with great success (Fig. 3 A) [33,34]. However, sometimes one-pot synthesis could not produce red/NIR C-dots or with desired optical properties, this is when stepwise synthesis comes to play [35,36]. For instance, Wen et al. obtained cyan-emissive C-dots by sarcosine and urea; to redshift the PL of the C-dots, a separate reaction of the obtained C-dots in the presence of lanthanide ions was required [37].

In addition to hydrothermal reactions, solvothermal treatments are also frequently applied for red/NIR C-dots preparation, the only difference between these two methods is the solvent used (Fig. 2C). Compared to hydrothermal reactions, solvothermal synthesis has several advantages: firstly, precursors with poor aqueous solubility that are not suitable for hydrothermal treatment could well fit for solvothermal reactions; secondly, solvothermal reactions could achieve much higher reaction temperatures by selecting solvents of high boiling points; lastly, doping elements such as nitrogen and sulfur could be directly introduced into C-dots from the organic solvents (i.e., dimethyl formamide, dimethyl sulfoxide) used (Fig. 3B) [38–41]. However, it's worth to mention, the water dispersibility of C-dots prepared by solvothermal synthesis is generally poor, and additional modifications of the C-dots might be necessary for applications requiring good aqueous availability.

Microwave irradiation is another commonly applied method for the synthesis of red/NIR C-dots, which could be carried out with a professional microwave synthesizer or domestic microwave ovens (Fig. 2D). Unlike hydrothermal/solvothermal reactions, where heat is transferred layer by layer from outside to inside via convection and conduction, and thus cause very low heat conduction efficiency; the microwave synthesis use microwave irradiations to heat the reactions directly, and thus leads to a much shorter reaction time [42–44].

Apart from the isolated use of hydrothermal treatment or microwave irradiation, the synthesis strategy of combining the above two methods was also an appreciable route, in which the microwave method act as role of pre-carbonization and the latter let the reaction sufficient. For instance, our group reported a kilogram-scale preparation of NIR C-dots from o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and the estimated cost for each gram of C-dots was only $ 0.1. With mere microwave irradiation, only yellow C-dots that emitted at 568 nm were obtained due to the insufficient carbonization. After the further hydrothermal treatment, NIR C-dots that emitted at 653 nm were successfully synthesized due to the increasing of carbonization degree (Fig. 3C) [45]. In addition, there are also some less commonly applied synthetic methods for red/NIR C-dots preparation. For example, Chen et al. obtained NIR C-dots by simply heating 1, 2, 4-triaminobenzene powder at 200 °C in an argon gas stream for 6 h, which was simple, straight forward and with high production yield (80 %) [46]. Recently, our group reported the precrystallization-tuned direct calcination of o-PD crystals, achieving the high efficiency synthesis of NIR C-dots at hector-gram scale [47]. As can be seen, synthesis of red/NIR C-dots through inheriting the structure and properties of proper molecular precursor is the most efficient strategies, thus limited the synthesis methods mainly to hydrothermal/solvothermal and microwave reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon precursors for Red/NIR C-dots preparation
ce:section.ce:para: As various studies have demonstrated, carbon precursors had significant impacts on the chemical, structural and optical properties of C-dots; in some special occasions, C-dots even resemble a great extent of the intrinsic nature of carbon precursors [21,48–53]. As such, the proper selection of carbon precursors is highly important for the successful synthesis of red/NIR C-dots. After one decade of efforts, researchers have successfully synthesized red/NIR C-dots from various precursors, among which, citric acid-, amines-, and phenols-derived red/NIR C-dots have been extremely mature. Besides, some specifically designed polymers, organic dyes, biomacromolecules as well as biomass have also been fruitful in preparing red/NIR C-dots (Fig. 4 ).

Citric acid (CA) is among the most commonly used small organic molecules for the synthesis of C-dots since it has rich presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups which are very suitable for polymerization and carbonization. As such, various groups have achieved red/NIR C-dots synthesis from CA by accompany with other small organic molecules such as ethylenediamine (EDA) [33,54,55], urea [38,40,56–62], thiourea [63], N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (AEAPMS) [64,65] and formamide [27,66] (Fig. 5 A).

Citric acid + Ethylenediamine: CA and EDA (Figs. 5A, 1) are commonly used for the facile preparation of C-dots, unfortunately most of the C-dots formed only emit in the blue to green region [67,68]. To achieve red/NIR emission, several strategies have been reported. The first one is the introduction of lanthanide ions to the system; as lanthanide ions are known to have various energy gaps that emit in the NIR region. For instance, Wu and co-workers successfully realized the tuning of blue-emissive C-dots from CA and EDA into NIR-emissive by adding LnCl3 into the one-pot hydrothermal reaction [33]. Unfortunately, this approach involves lanthanide elements, which are not only rare and expensive, but may also reduce the biocompatibility of the C-dots formed. Thus, continuous efforts have been devoted for strategies that do not require lanthanide doping. Mechanistically speaking, both carboxyl (from CA) and amino (from EDA) groups are hydrophilic and thus could be dispersed in water very well in a hydrothermal reaction, which is not favorable for the formation of C-dots nucleus. On the other hand, the hydrophilic groups from CA and EDA are more likely to attract each other and form the carbon core with large size in solvothermal reactions, which should significantly red-shift the emissions of C-dots formed. Indeed, by replacing water with formamide as the reaction medium, Ding and co-workers successfully prepared C-dots that emitted at 627 nm, which are much red-shifted than their counterparts prepared in water (Fig. 5B) [54].

Citric acid + Urea: Similar to CA and EDA, CA and urea are also commonly applied for the synthesis of red/NIR C-dots. In addition to its amino groups, urea also contains a carbonyl group (Figs. 5A, 2 ), which could undergo nucleophilic addition reactions, making its reaction pathways with CA more versatile. Since the polymerization of CA and urea could be regarded as an acid-catalyzed process, the amount of CA used in the synthesis would have a significant influence on the properties of C-dots. Indeed, Yang and co-workers demonstrated that with increased amount of CA, the emissions of C-dots synthesized from CA and urea gradually shifted from blue, green to red [56].

Besides, researchers also found that CA and urea have the difficulty to nucleate to form carbon core in aqueous solutions, thus the nature of the reaction medium has great impacts on the synthesis and properties of C-dots. To explore this effect, a series of studies have been carried out, among which, Qu's group has made a big stride [38,40,57–62]! They had revealed that aprotic solvents such as DMF and DMSO could provide higher degree of dehydration and carbonization of C-dots than the protic solvents such as water, resulting in the gradually increased sp2-domains and red-shifted absorption [38,40,57]. Through tuning the volume ratio of water, glycerol and DMF, Qu's group successfully achieved the preparation of C-dots emitting in blue to red spectral range from CA and urea [60].

In addition to the variations in their solvent proton self-delivery ability, the different elements presented in the various solvents could also influence the PL properties of C-dots. Compared to DMF, DMSO could introduce sulfur that could insert new energy levels to the orbitals in both excited and ground states, thus the C-dots obtained in DMSO are closer to the NIR region. For instance, Qu's group dissolved CA and urea in DMSO with 160 °C for 4 h treatment [38]. The as-prepared C-dots showed strong NIR emissions under 655 nm excitation thanks to the lowered optical bandgaps by S-doping. Inspired by this study, Li et al. obtained high quality red-emissive C-dots by CA and thiourea (Figs. 5A, 3), in which thiourea could provide enough sulfur for the system while urea failed to (Fig. 5C) [63]. In addition to the above-discussed strategies, surface charge engineering such as hydroxide/hydrochloric treatment could also be applied to reduce the overlap between PL emissions and absorptions, and thus achieving efficient red-emission.

Citric acid + Other Chemicals: In addition to EDA and urea, synthesis of red/NIR C-dots using CA with other chemicals including AEAPMS and formamide has also been reported. In 2015, Sun et al. reported C-dos obtained by CA and AEAPMS (Figs. 5A, 4), which emitted at 620 nm with 540 nm excitation [64]. Later in 2017, He and co-workers synthesized red emissive C-dots using the same reactants with slight modification in the synthesis strategy, which had an optimum excitation at 580 nm and an optimum emission peak at 640 nm (Fig. 5D). The organosilane-functionalized C-dots showed unique solid state emission due to the FRET process [65]. In addition to AEAPMS, formamide was also applied to couple with CA to synthesize red/NIR C-dots, which indicated that formamide functioned not only as the reaction medium, but also as the reactant and passivation agent [27,66,69]. For instance, Pan et al. using CA and formamide (Fig. 5A, 5) as precursors successfully synthesized NIR C-dots via microwave-assisted method, which exhibited a wide range of emissions crossing 400–800 nm [27].

In addition to CA, amines are another type of precursors that have been widely used for the synthesis of red/NIR C-dots. The use of both alkyl and aromatic amines has been reported, among them, reports using aromatic amines (i.e., phenylenediamines) have dominated. Alkyl amines used for red/NIR C-dots preparation generally contain at least two amino groups for facile polymerization with polyacids; representative alkyl amines of such include EDA (Fig. 6 A, 1). Synthesis of red/NIR C-dots from EDA is generally accompanied with CA as discussed above.

Aromatic amines (Figs. 6A, 2–8) are very suitable for synthesis of red/NIR C-dots, because the self-polymerization of these aromatic units would result in highly π-conjugated systems with narrow band gaps. Indeed, as the commonly seen aniline derivatives, phenylenediamines (PDs, Figs. 6A, 2–4) have been extremely successful as carbon precursors for the synthesis of NIR C-dots. Back in 2015, Jiang et al. reported a study in which they successfully obtained C-dots that emitted blue, green, and red from m-PD, o-PD and p-PD, respectively (Fig. 6B) [70]. Later in 2019, they successfully prepared multicolor C-dots through the same synthesis method with the addition of tartaric acid (TA), which increased the surface oxidation and carboxylation of the as-prepared C-dots [71]. Notably, C-dots derived from o-PD and TA exhibited a multi-peak emission covering the region from red to NIR (Fig. 6C).

Inspired by these reports, continuous attention has been attracted by this class of precursors for the preparation of red/NIR C-dots, and enormous advancements have been achieved [45,72–80]. For instance, Jia et al. synthesized three red/NIR emissive, electron-donating group passivated C-dots with high PLQYs from N,N-dimethyl-, N,N-diethyl-, and N,N-dipropyl-p-PD (Figs. 6A, 5) [72]. Li and co-workers obtained blue, green and red fluorescent folate-receptor-targeting C-dots from folic acid and PDs [73]. Liu et al. synthesized high efficient red emissive C-dots from o-PD and HNO3 with the optimal emission at about 630 nm and a shoulder peak at 677 nm [74]. Recently, our group prepared NIR C-dots from o-PD and H2SO4 with an optimal emission at 653 nm [45]. Careful study showed that two molecules of o-PD could self-condense to form 2, 3-diaminophenazine (DAP) in acidic condition, and the DAP molecules could further polymerize to form dimers upon microwave irradiation. These dimers were thermodynamically unstable and could further polymerize and cross link with each other to form the stable NIR C-dots after in situ hydrothermal treatment (Fig. 6D). To overcome the disadvantages associated with the microwave-assisted hydrothermal reaction, the same group further optimized the process of NIR C-dots preparation [47]. Specifically, the possibility of crystalline pre-treatment of o-PD in the presence of acid was investigated, followed by direct calcination of o-PD crystals to fabricate NIR C-dots. This “pre-crystallization-carbonization” process effectively avoided complex purification procedures caused by impurities generated from disordered aggregation in solvent systems, rendering a safer and more efficient method for large-scale production of NIR C-dots (Fig. 6E).

Besides the various acid additions, metal elements doping has also been effective in promoting the formation of red/NIR C-dots from PDs. For instance, Xia et al. used hydrothermal method to prepare red-emissive C-dots by adding FeCl3 to p-PDs at 80 °C [81]. The addition of FeCl3 reduced the temperature required and catalyzed the carbonized core formation through catalytic polymerization. In a different study, Liu et al. prepared red/NIR C-dots via an in situ solvent-free carbonization strategy from o-PD with the addition of aluminum chloride hexahydrate, which successfully shifted the absorption of C-dots to the long wavelength region [82]. Specifically, the PL spectra of the as-prepared C-dots exhibited three obvious peaks at 590, 640 and 700 nm with a tail extending to 900 nm under 532 nm laser illumination.

While the metal ions (i.e., Fe3+, Al3+) above functioned as Lewis acids to catalyze the reaction, in some studies metal ions also played as coordinating ions in the formation of red/NIR C-dots. For instance, Hua et al. compared C-dots prepared from p-PDs and different metal ions including silver, copper, platinum, iron, palladium and nickel [83]. Their study shown that C-dots derived from Ni2+ and p-PD emitted light up to 700 nm and exhibited the highest QYs among all the C-dots, which was attributed to the Ni2+—p-PD networks resulted from the strong coordination interaction of Ni2+ with p-PD. In addition to PDs, various PDs derivatives such as 1,2,4-triaminobenzene (Fig. 6A, 6) [84], 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (Figs. 6A, 7) [85], tris(4-aminophenyl)amine (TAPA, Figs. 6A, 8) [86] and etc [87–90]. have also been reported for the synthesis of red/NIR C-dots.

Similar to aromatic amines, phenols also have great potential to synthesize red/NIR C-dots since they could go through dehydrative condensation to form highly conjugated π systems with narrow band gaps [91]. For instance, Zhu et al. prepared multicolor phenol-derived C-dots via solvothermal treatment and C-dots with red emission were obtained by using 1,3-naphthalenediol and N-bromosuccinimide as carbon precursors thanks to the introduction of halogen atoms (Fig. 7 A) [92].

In the hope to increase the reactivity of phenol condensation as well as obtain conjugates with controlled growth, some phenol derivatives such as hydroquinone and naphthalenediol have been exploited. For instance, Fan and co-workers successfully prepared red-emitting C-dots with a high QY (53 %) using 1, 3-naphthalenediol (Fig. 7B) by a sequential dehydrative condensation and dehydrogenative planarization approach [93]. In the method, one molecule of 1,3-dihydroxynaphthalene could be considered as the smallest sp2 domain with –OH groups, which concomitantly acted as a building block to form large sized conjugated sp2 cluster with –OH groups at the edge sites. Based on this work, the same group successfully synthesized triangular-shaped NIR C-dots from phloroglucinol and resorcinol (Fig. 7C) [94]. The selection of carbon precursor in this study was very delicate; the three meta-positioned hydrogens are activated due to the presence of the three electron-donating hydroxyl groups in the same molecule, resulting the highly organized self-condensation of hydroquinone triglycan. By carefully tuning the reaction parameters, a series of triangular C-dots with different sizes and emissions were successfully prepared. Furthermore, synthesis of NIR C-dots from a similar precursor, 1, 8-naphthalenediol, has also been reported for the detection of water in organic solvents [95].

In addition to small-organic-molecule carbon precursors, polymer-based carbon precursors have also been heavily investigated recently. Polymers generally have clear, repeatable and controllable structural features; thus, they are very suitable for C-dots synthesis, mechanism investigation and PL tuning [96]. Among the various studies, Ge et al. made a big stride in the preparation of red/NIR C-dots from polymers [28,97–100]. According to literature precedents, they hypothesized that C-dots prepared from well-defined molecular precursors might give NIR emissions. Indeed, C-dots prepared from polythiophene phenylpropionic acid (PPA, a conjugated polymer) demonstrated superior PL properties (Fig. 7D) [28]. The as-prepared C-dots exhibited a red emission peak at 640 nm, which could be applied not only as a red light imaging probe but also as an effective PDT/PTT therapy agent. The following year, the same team further synthesized polythiophene benzoic acid (PBA) as a carbon source to prepare C-dots (Fig. 7E) with emissions ranging from 500 nm to NIR region [99]. During the carbonization process, sheet-like polymers were cut into smaller sheet-like graphite and further broken into C-dots. Inspired by these findings, the same group further realized the preparation of a series of tunable multicolor C-dots from the mixture of different well-defined polythiophene derivatives; and the PL of these multicolor C-dots could be facilely tuned by manipulating the N and O doping amount which was closely related to the ratio of the polythiophene derivatives (Fig. 7F) [100].

In a different work, Huang et al. accomplished the facile tuning of C-dots PL by simply adjusting the lengths of the side alkyl chains of the carbon precursor polythiophene derivatives, which was of significance to the structural design of C-dots [101]. Thanks to these contributions, red/NIR C-dots derived from polythiophene has been well developed. Later in 2017, Lan et al. further advanced this field by synthesizing Se-doped C-dots with NIR emissions at 731 and 820 nm using polythiophene derivatives and Se-containing carbon source [34]. Significantly, the C-dots exhibited a photothermal conversion efficiency of ∼58.2 %, which was the highest of C-dots at that time and could compete with Au nanostructures. In 2021, Jin et al. also prepared C-dots from polythiophene through chemical cutting by nitric acid and discovered that the red PL was mainly governed by sp2 domains, and the degree of surface oxidation was a secondary factor [102].

Organic NIR chromophores such as cyanine dyes have been extensively explored as probes for the imaging diagnosis and PTT treatment of cancer. However, their application is greatly limited due to the low photostability, poor water solubility and inferior bioavailability (i.e., rapid blood clearance) [103,104]. In such context, carbonization of cyanine dyes has become an alternative choice as it could obtain the advantages of C-dots while retaining its own properties [21,31,105–108]. For instance, Zheng et al. reported a new strategy to fabricate multifunctional C-dots for theranostic development directly from such conventional organic dyes (CyOH) [31]. The as-prepared C-dots exhibited desired properties such as good water solubility and excellent photostability while still maintaining a high photothermal conversion efficiency (38.7 %) and NIR emission, which facilitated their application as theranostic agent for simultaneous NIR imaging and photothermal therapy in vitro and in vivo. In a different study, Yang et al. synthesized a new type of NIR C-dots (Cy7-CDs) derived from Cy7 cyanine dye and PEG [105]. The as-prepared Cy7-CDs showed low toxicity and good biocompatibility, which could detect heavy metal ions Au3+/Hg2+ and glutathione in an “on-off-on” mode. Compared with raw material, Cy7-CDs could target mitochondria and detect analytes with high selectivity and sensitivity. Recently, our group also reported a simple strategy to enhance the properties of indocyanine green (ICG) via a one-pot hydrothermal method [21]. After facile carbonization, the as-prepared ICGCDs not only inherited the characteristics of ICG, but also refrained from its shortcomings (i.e., poor chemical and optical stability, mediocre photothermal properties) (Fig. 8 A). Specifically, ICGCDs maintained essentially the same photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) even after 10 cycles as indicated by the unaltered temperature peaks while the photothermal performances of ICG began to quickly deteriorate from the second cycle under repeated laser irradiation, and only 57.3 % of the original PCE was retained in the fifth cycle (Fig. 8B). The stability differences of ICG and ICGCDs in the cyclability test could also be visually differentiated. Inspired by this work, Domena et al. reported the synthesis of C-dots derived from ICG, CA and PEG1000 with NIR emission [108]. The as-prepared C-dots could achieve red imaging in glioblastoma stem-like cells and crossing the blood-brain barrier (BBB) in zebrafish.

Biomass could be obtained easily from nature and biomass-derived C-dots generally demonstrate high water solubility, low cytotoxicity and superior environment friendliness [109–111]. Significantly, biomass is rich of chemical components with various elements (i.e., N, S, P, etc.), which are ideal precursors for the preparation of red/NIR C-dots with controllable PL properties [112–117]. For instance, Li et al. used watermelon juice as sole precursor and prepared a new type of NIR-II C-dots through hydrothermal treatment. The NIR-II emissions of the C-dots were ascribed to the high degree of surface oxidation and N doping; exploiting this property, the C-dots were successfully demonstrated as an effective probe for in vivo NIR imaging [112]. In a different study, Cui et al. prepared NIR C-dots from black pepper, the emission of which could reach as far as 668 nm (Fig. 8C) [113]. The NIR emission of the C-dots was attributed to the nitrogen doping due to the rich presence of N-containing chemicals (i.e., alkaloids, porphyrins, etc.) of black pepper. In a recent report, Liu et al. synthesized a strong NIR C-dots derived from mulberry leaves (Fig. 8D) with a sharp and shoulder emission peak at 676 and 725 nm, respectively [114]. After feeding silkworms with the obtained C-dots, the cocoons produced by silkworms exhibited bright red fluorescence under UV light, which provided a new approach for the fluorescent silk preparation. Similarly, Yang and co-workers prepared high-performance deep red emissive C-dots with taxus leaves (Fig. 8E) [115]. The as-prepared deep red C-dots showed the narrowest full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 20 nm and highest QY (31 %) for deep red/NIR fluorescent C-dots under the deep red light excitation.

In addition to biomass-derived red/NIR C-dots, C-dots directly prepared from biomolecules extracted or isolated from biomass have also been reported [118–123]. Similar to biomass-derived C-dots, these biomolecules-based C-dots also exhibited superior biocompatibility and thus were frequently applied in the biomedical fields. For instance, Wen et al. synthesized NIR C-dots with maximum emission at 680 nm from pheophytin, which exhibited a high singlet oxygen generation rate [118]. In a different study, Yang et al. obtained NIR C-dots from reduced glutathione and formamide, which also exhibited excellent biocompatibility and were successfully applied for high-resolution cellular imaging and cortisol detection [119].

In addition to the common organics-derived red/NIR C-dots syntheses, a few inorganic carbon precursors have also been reported to result in red/NIR C-dots [9,26,124,125]. For instance, Liu et al. used strong acid to oxidize the smoke from incomplete candle burning and obtained C-dots that emitted from 415 to 615 nm [9]. Similarly, Lee et al. synthesized NIR C-dots through a “top-down” method by oxidizing reduced graphene oxide of high porosity [124]. Under an 808 nm laser excitation, the as-prepared C-dots exhibited NIR fluorescence spanning the wavelength range of 850–1100 nm with an optimal emission peak at 900 nm. In a different study, Liu et al. obtained a series of C-dots emitting from blue to red by electrolyzing graphite rods and doping with different metals (Ag, Au, and Cu) [26]. Selective examples using different precursors for the preparation of red/NIR C-dots were summarized below in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence mechanism and optical tuning for Red/NIR C-dots

ce:section.ce:para: The emission mechanism of C-dots is still lack of a unified explanation and the ambiguous understanding prevents the controlled synthesis of red/NIR C-dots. Currently, there are mainly four models to explain the PL mechanisms of C-dots that endorsed by most researchers, namely core-state luminescence [126–131], surface-state luminescence [132–137], molecular-state luminescence [138–140] and crosslinking-enhanced emission (CEE) [81,141–145]. With the gradual deepening of research on the PL mechanism of C-dots, the luminescence phenomenon of C-dots appeared to be a complex result influenced by multiple effects, rather than a simple, isolated mechanism [146–150]. Even so, the exploration and understanding of the basic PL mechanism models for C-dots are still of significance for the proper modulation of C-dots PL properties [151].

Core-state luminescence: Core-state emissions are mainly attributed to the quantum confinement effect and the size effect of the π-π conjugating domains, which are generally formed by the aromatic rings of the carbon core. By expanding the sizes and ranges of π-π conjugating domains of C-dots, the energy gaps of C-dots could be gradually decreased, leading to a red shift in the PL emissions. Back in 2010, Eda et al. systematically investigated the relationship between the sizes and transition energies of C-dots using density-functional theory (DFT) and the result showed that as the conjugating sizes enlarged, the required transition energy decreased accordingly (Fig. 9 A) [128]. Later, other researchers came to a similar conclusion that the luminescence properties of C-dots is closely related to the carbon core structure [129]. In a different study, Mahasin et al. also reached the similar conclusion with the aid of theoretical calculations that the fluorescence of C-dots could originate due to the quantum confinement effect of sp2 conjugated π electrons, and was easily influenced by the size, edge structure, and shape of the sp2 conjugating domains [130]. To experimentally probe the core-state emissions, Kim et al. fabricated a series of C-dots with varied sizes and found that the PL behaviors of these C-dots were indeed size-dependent and also influenced by the corresponding edge variations (Fig. 9B) [131].

Surface-state luminescence: Generally speaking, defects due to the incorporation of heteroatoms and/or the introduction of functionalities are densely presented on the surface of C-dots, and every surface defect of such could serve as the capture center for exciton and thus result in surface-state related emissions [132,133]. Specifically, defects at the edges of the core structures could introduce new energy levels and thus decrease the differences between HOMO and LUMO energy levels. As a result, C-dots emissions are generally red shifted as surface defects concentrated. In 2016, Xiong's group prepared series of multicolor C-dots with emissions ranging from 440 to 625 nm [134], and the redshift of the emissions were attributed to the gradual increased surface oxidation degree, which could cause significant local distortions and thus narrow the energy gaps (Fig. 9C). Worth mention, it is believed that surface defects could generally lead to non-radiative transitions, yielding lowered PLQYs [135]. Hence, it is of great importance to delicately balance the defects concentration on C-dots surface [136,137].

Molecular-state luminescence: In a typical molecular-state PL, the emissions of C-dots are attributed to the presence of molecular fluorophores. These fluorescent chromophores are formed during the carbonization of precursors and attached or incorporated to in-situ formed C-dots. As such, C-dots dominated by molecular-state luminescence could generally retain characteristics of carbon precursors, endowing them with highly desired properties [152–155]. Among the various C-dots, those prepared from small molecules such as CA and amines are prone to demonstrate molecular-state-dominated fluorescence. For the first time, Yang's group synthesized blue-emissive C-dots of high QYs from CA and EDA, and systematically investigated their PL emission mechanism, in which the molecular-state-dominated PL was attributed to the formation of blue fluorescent chromophore IPCA (Fig. 9D) [138]. In a different study, Schneider et al. prepared three C-dots from CA and three different nitrogen sources (ethylenediamine, hexamethylenetetramine, and triethanol-amine) [140]. Systematic investigation also highlighted the strong impact of molecular fluorophores on the PL of C-dots obtained.

Cross-linking enhanced emission: Cross-linking enhanced emission (CEE) could be regarded as a special type of luminescence mechanism mainly for CPDs, which was first proposed by Yang's group back in 2014 (Fig. 9E) [143]. CPDs generally have potential sub-fluorescent groups, and the internal vibration and rotation of these sub-fluorescent groups could be limited upon aggregation and cross-linking, forcing this portion of mechanical energy released in the form of luminescence. Using branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) as precursors, the authors confirmed that the decreased vibration and rotation of cross-linked PEI contributed significantly to the PL enhancing. Later, the same group also demonstrated the presence of CEE emissions in a polyacrylic acid (PPA) and EDA derived PDs (Fig. 9F) [144].

Heteroatom doping: Heteroatom doping is among the most common approach to tune C-dots PL, which could be facilely done during or after the C-dots synthesis [156]. Nitrogen (N), sulfur (S) and oxygen (O) are the most commonly used heteroatoms for doping, which could cause lattice distortion and thus adjust the electronic structure of C-dots [156–158]. N atoms are versatile as regarding the formation of N-containing groups of different oxidation and conjugation states (i.e., pyrrolic, pyridinic and graphitic nitrogens), and thus N-containing precursors are frequently adopted for the modulation of C-dots PL [159–164]. For instance, Liu et al. reported the lowering of C-dots electronic bandgaps from 2.01 to 0.64 eV by N doping (Fig. 10 A), which successfully shifted the emissions to the long wavelength region and enhanced the PLQY [162]. In a different study, Ye et al. reported graphitic-nitrogen-content controlled tuning of C-dots PL [163], in which increased graphitic nitrogen could narrow HOMO-LUMO gap and thus lead to the redshift of fluorescent emissions (Fig. 10B). Recently, Domena et al. reported that C-dots with lower nitrogen content demonstrated fewer surface defects presence, which resulted higher energy band gaps [164].

In addition to regular optical property tuning as described above, rare earth elements doping could directly endow C-dots with multiple emission properties, in which C-dots could act as “antenna” to sensitize rare earth metal ions with red/NIR characteristic luminescence. For instance, Zhang et al. reported Yb3+-doped C-dots exhibiting both fluorescence and phosphorescence, and the doping of Yb3+ was believed to be the key for the occurrence of phosphorescence (Fig. 10C) [165]. Interestingly, some metal ions could play a role similar to a “catalyst” in the formation of C-dots. For instance, Hua et al. found that although the obtained C-dots were metal-free, their PL emissions were still heavily influenced by the initially added doping metal ions [83].

Tuning of reaction parameters: Polarity of solvent was a key parameter for the synthesis of C-dots as it could control the dehydration and carbonization of precursors, thus influencing the sizes and π-π conjugating domains of C-dots. In a reaction system with mixed solvents, the polarity of the solvents could be facilely manipulated by altering the solvent ratios. For instance, Qu and co-workers achieved the preparation of full-color C-dots from the same precursor and reaction by simply adjusting the ratios of the solvents (water, glycerol, and DMF) (Fig. 10D) [60]. Specifically, the dehydration and carbonization degree of reactions in water, glycerol and DMF were gradually increased, resulting in the increased sp2-domains and redshift of absorption and emission bands. In a different study, Ding et al. synthesized NIR C-dots with an excitation-independent PL emission at 704 nm by heating the formamide solution of pulp-free lemon juice; and the optical properties could be facilely tuned by manipulating the dosage of formamide [166].

In addition to polarity tuning, the solvent pH could also influence the dehydration and carbonization degree of carbon precursors. For instance, Xiong's group obtained a series of NIR C-dots by treating l-glutamic acid and o-PDs with sulfuric acid [167]. Control experiments shown that as the concentrations of sulfuric acid increased from 0.06 to 8.2 mol/L, the sizes and graphitization degree of C-dots also gradually increased, leading to redshift of C-dots PL emissions from 581 to 746 nm. Recently, Lin's group reported the synthesis of red, green and blue emissive C-dots from m-hydroxybenzaldehyde which took different reaction paths under neutral, alkaline, and acidic conditions, respectively [168]. Specifically, base acted on the hydroxyl group while acid acted on the aldehyde group of the precursor during the reaction, leading to the formation of fluorescent groups with different conjugated structures (Fig. 10E).

Apart from the tuning of reaction solvent, manipulation of the reaction time is also a commonly adopted approach to tune the degree of carbonization and polymerization, thereby adjusting the size and luminescence of C-dots. For instance, in the preparation of red C-dots from the FeCl3-promoted polymerization of p-PD by Xia et al. it was shown that the morphology, structure and PL emissions of C-dots were closely related to the reaction time [81]. In a similar study, Yuan et al. also reported the tuning of C-dots PL emissions via the manipulation of reaction time [85].

C-dots structural design: The PL of C-dots was heavily influenced by surface- and core-state emissions, as such, surface functionality and core structure design could be a powerful tool for the tuning of PL properties of red/NIR C-dots. Functional groups (i.e., amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl group) could directly determine the surface states of C-dots and adjust their PL by tuning the surface energy levels [169–175]. For instance, Jia et al. synthesized three red-emitting, electron-donating group passivated C-dots (R-EGP-CDs) using N, N-dimethyl-, N, N-diethyl-, and N, N-dipropyl-p-PD, in which the –NR2 groups were directly attached to the π-conjugating domains (Fig. 11 A) [72]. Compared to the C-dots (QY = 26 %; emitted at 604 nm) of p-PD synthesized by Jiang et al. back in 2015 [70], these C-dots demonstrated much higher QYs (77.9–86.0 %) and longer emissions (637–645 nm), which highlighted the feasibility of tuning C-dots PL properties via the controlling and manipulation of C-dots surface functionalities. In a recent study, Lin's group reported a strategy to synthesize multicolor solid-state emissive C-dots with CA as carbon source and six alkyl amines as the carbonization solvents [175]. The amine groups of the solvent could react with the carboxyl group of CA forming the carbon core while the alkyl chain substituents at the other end of the amine solvents were attached to the surfaces of C-dots. With aromatic alkyl chains, the aromatic structure at the end of the substituent caused extension of the electron system, which is similar to an increase in the π−π conjugating domains, resulting in redshift of the emissions (Fig. 11B), again highlighting the practicality of surface functionality manipulation for C-dots PL tuning.

In addition to the above-discussed classical surface functionality manipulation, protonation and deprotonation could be regarded as a special scenario of surface functionality tuning. Protonation process could be regarded as the electron-donating groups attracting protons and deprotonation is the process of removing protons from molecules and generating corresponding conjugate bases. Generally speaking, protonation could reduce the energy bandwidth and result in the redshift of C-dots PL emissions. For instance, Zhang et al. reported that o-PD derived C-dots exhibited one-photon PL emission at 550 nm in neutral condition, and the emission was readily redshifted to 620 nm with dramatically narrowed peak widths upon protonation [176]. The shift was attributed to the protonation of the amino (-NH2) groups of 2, 3-diaminophenazine, which narrowed the photon transition bandgap (Fig. 11C). Worth to mention, in addition to the tuning of bandgaps (and thus the PL emissions); in some occasions, surface protons could also trap and stabilize the excited electrons of C-dots to some degree, thus influence the PLQYs and florescent lifetime of C-dots. For instance, our group reported the kilogram-scale synthesis of NIR C-dots from o-PD which were extremely sensitive to surface protonation/deprotonation [45]: the maximum emission peak was readily redshifted from 606 to 653 nm after protonation, while the PLQYs were decreased from 25.4 % to 8.1 % and fluorescent lifetime was increased from 1.38 to 2.38 ns (Fig. 11D).

In addition to surface functionality tuning, effective carbon core structure design could also lead to redshift of C-dots PL by extending the conjugation domains [70,101,130,131,177–179]. For instance, Jiang et al. used m-, o-, p-PDs to synthesize C-dots that emitted blue, green, and red emissions, respectively [70]. Due to the different positions of amino groups in PD monomers, the difficulty of joining each other in the synthesis of C-dots was different, which resulted in different C-dots sizes, thereby influencing the PL emissions of C-dots. In a different study, Huang et al. synthesized deep red to short-wave NIR C-dots from polythiophene derivatives, which mainly comprised a polythiophene skeleton and alkyl chains [101]. By adjusting the lengths of the alkyl side chains, the conjugation structure of carbon core could be effectively extended during the carbonization reaction, thus red shifting the PL emissions.

Thanks to their superior water dispersibility, low biological toxicity and excellent PL properties, red/NIR C-dots have been widely applied in various biological fields, and bioimaging is among the most important applications [180–183]. Following the signals transmitted by C-dots, real-time situation in biological tissues could be clearly observed and tracked. Depending on the signals emitted by C-dots, they could be applied for fluorescent imaging, photoacoustic imaging, magnetic resonance imaging, as well as multi-modal imaging in which multiple signals are working together.

Fluorescent imaging: Fluorescent imaging is generally achieved by receiving light signals emitted by C-dots and could be mainly divided into in vitro and in vivo modes. The former could be used to determine the cell uptake and imaging capabilities of C-dots. For instance, Pan et al. synthesized C-dots with NIR emission (683 nm) under either 420 or 850 nm of laser excitation [184]. After incubated with MCF-7 cells, the deep red emission from cells could be clearly observed by both single-photon and two-photon mode in the confocal imaging (Fig. 12 A), highlighting the feasibility of C-dots as a single-photon and two-photon bioimaging agent. Recently, Bi and co-workers reported C-dots with targeted imaging capability of RNA and stress granules (SGs) via microwave treatment of neutral red and levofloxacin [185]. The as-prepared C-dots exhibited bright red (λmax = 642 nm, QY = 22.83 %) emission, which was used to track the cellular internalization of C-dots, and the RNA related regions in cells could be selectively “light up”.

In addition to in vitro cellular imaging, the in vivo fluorescent imaging of red/NIR C-dots that could obtain information about tumor growth and metastasis in living animals has also attracted significant attention. Ascribed to the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect towards tumor, nano-sized C-dots generally have passive targeting effects to tumors, leading to obvious accumulations at the tumor area which could be facilely observed by tracking the in vivo C-dots PL emissions (Fig. 12B) [31]. To enhance the specific-cancer-targeting capability of C-dots, various targeting moieties including small molecules and peptides have been incorporated onto C-dots to provide active targeting [186–192]. For instance, taking advantage of the high expression of folate receptors on tumors, Zhong et al. designed and constructed folic acid (FA) functionalized red C-dots (FA-CDs), which exhibited obvious accumulation at the tumor site in vivo which the bare C-dots failed to achieve [192]. Interestingly, although C-dots functionalized with targeting ligands such as transferrin, hyaluronic acid, aptamer and peptide [193–196] have been widely reported, red/NIR C-dots with these ligands for active cancer targeting were rarely reported.

Photoacoustic imaging: Generally speaking, C-dots with red/NIR light absorption and photothermal conversion capacity could elevate the internal temperature under laser irradiation, which would cause the expansion and contraction of local structure, and thus radiate acoustic signals outward. Exploiting this phenomenon, photoacoustic imaging agents were developed to reconstruct organ/tissue images on the basis of these ultrasound signals. Thanks to the relatively strong tissue penetration capability of ultrasound signals, imaging of deep biological tissues could be generally achieved. Worth mention, most diseases exhibit abnormal photoacoustic contrast in early stage, thus photoacoustic contrast agents have great potential for early disease diagnosis. As a result, studies on the development of red/NIR C-dots-based photoacoustic contrast agents have received a great deal of attention in the last decade [28,38,197–201]. For instance, in 2015, Ge et al. synthesized NIR C-dots from polythiophene benzene propionic acid which could produce acoustic signals under laser excitation (710 nm) and the intensity of the acoustic signal demonstrated a good linear relationship with the concentration of C-dots (Fig. 12C) [28]. Recently, Han et al. also reported the application of C-dots for photoacoustic imaging, in which stable photoacoustic signals could be detected from mouse tumors in vivo from 2 to 24 h after injection (Fig. 12D), highlighting the great potential of red/NIR C-dots as photoacoustic contrast agent [201].

Multi-modal imaging: Integrating advantages of multiple imaging modes, multi-modal imaging has been gradually developed by utilizing multiple signals to construct imaging. Among the various possibilities [197,202–204], multi-modal imaging taking advantage of fluorescent and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has been mostly investigated due to their easy access [205–210]. In principle, C-dots doped with MRI-active elements (i.e., Fe, Co and Ni) could readily realize the simultaneous fluorescent and MRI imaging. For example, Li et al. prepared Fe3O4-composited C-dots, which not only demonstrated the super paramagnetic properties of Fe3O4 nanocrystals for MRI contrast, but also manifested the compelling PL properties of C-dots [209]. In a different study, Zhang and co-workers constructed novel multifunctional PDA@N-CDs (Mn) NPs from polydopamine and manganese ions (Mn2+) [210]. Thanks to the obvious NIR absorbance and high photothermal efficiency (28.2 %) of bare C-dots and the magnetic resonance performance provided by Mn2+, the multifunctional composite achieved tri-modal fluorescent, photothermal and magnetic resonance imaging.

Drug delivery systems (DDS) could improve the bioavailability (i.e., water solubility) and internal circulation capacity of drugs as well as reducing their side effects, thus have been extensively studied. Due to the rich presence of surface functional groups such as amino and carboxyl, anti-cancer drugs could be loaded onto C-dots via covalent bonding [193,211–213], which had a significant influence on the therapeutic effects. For instance, Leblanc's group investigated three different covalent drug loading methods, namely direct covalent loading, hydrazinobenzoic acid mediated loading and -S-S- mediated loading, on the performance of doxorubicin (DOX) DDS (Fig. 13 A) [213]. Detailed study shown that the –S-S- mediated DDS exhibited the highest percentage of DOX release and anti-cancer efficacy in vitro.

In addition to the strong interaction of covalent bonding, facile π-π stacking and electrostatic interaction have been also widely applied for the construction of C-dots DDS [98,214–218]. For instance, Fan and co-workers successfully designed a DDS that was capable of delivering DOX specifically to the nuclei of breast cancer stem cells; in this system, DOX was noncovalently loaded onto red C-dots via π-π stacking [218]. In a different study, Ren et al. also constructed red C-dots based DDS that significantly enhanced the therapeutic effect of coptisine [98]. In this system, the positively charged drug coptisine was noncovalently bounded to the negatively charged C-dots via electrostatic attractions.

In a typical photothermal therapy (PTT), photosensitizers (PSs) such as precious metal nanomaterials [219], NIR organic dyes [220] and carbon-based nanomaterials [221–224] could efficiently convert absorbed light into heat, quickly elevate regional temperature and thus realize the eradication of tumor tissues. Although precious metal nanomaterials exhibit highest photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE), their potential toxicity has greatly limited their further applications. Besides, NIR organic dyes are often criticized due to their instability and rapid internal clearance. As such, carbon-based nanomaterials, especially red/NIR C-dots, have attracted increasing attention due to their excellent bioavailability, biocompatibility and photostability in recent years [21,34,38,225–227]. For instance, Lan et al. reported the preparation of NIR S, Se-co-doped C-dots with a high PCE of 58.2 % (Fig. 13B), which were found to be very effective in elevating regional temperature around tumors and eradicating tumorous tissues (Fig. 13C) [34]. In a different study, Bao et al. also reported NIR C-dots with a high PCE of 59.2 %, which was among the highest efficiencies for the carbon-based nanomaterials and other inorganic nanoparticles [38]. Recently, our group reported the synthesis of NIR C-dots with significantly enhanced properties via the simple carbonization of indocyanine green (ICG) [21]. It was found that the PCE of the as-prepared C-dots had a 50 % enhancement over the carbon precursor ICG. Most importantly, the ICG-derived C-dots demonstrated much better photothermal killing efficiency towards cancer cells in both in vitro and in vivo models (Fig. 13D). Generally speaking, to realize the ideal PTT effect, high concentrations of NIR C-dots and high-power NIR laser irradiation are required, which could cause damage to epidermal tissue. Hence, future studies should focus more on the enhancement of C-dots PCE when developing them as PTT agents.

In a typical photodynamic therapy (PDT), reactive oxygen species (ROSs) instead of heat are generated by PSs upon excited by light to oxidize and kill cancer cells [228]. Thanks to the excellent light absorption ability, C-dots could effectively transfer transition state electrons to surrounding oxygen molecules, rendering them as suitable PSs for PDT [10,118,229–232]. For instance, Lan et al. reported the preparation of NIR C-dots that were capable of generating ROSs from both singlet and triplet states, resulting in a ROS quantum yield of 1.3. Furthermore, the C-dots were very effective in eradicating HeLa cells photo dynamically, causing the shrinkage of cells and the formation of numerous blebs [10]. In a different study, Wen and co-workers also reported the synthesis of NIR C-dots with a ROS quantum yield of 0.62 from pheophytin [118], which could completely inhibit the tumor growth of tumor-bearing mice after irradiation by laser at 671 nm. Recently, Li et al. prepared C-dots with intrinsic NIR emission at 680 nm, which not only realized the long-term in vivo persistent bioimaging, but also acted as a ROS producer to induce promoted PDT (Fig. 13E) [232].

As discussed above, PTT was known for their efficiency in destroying tumor tissues by elevating environment temperature, however, the tumor ablation temperature (45 °C) will also lead to the death of normal cells around the tumor site [233,234], causing significant side effects. On the other hand, mild PTT could restrain damage to normal tissues; however, the corresponding cancer cell killing capability is also greatly affected. Similarly, PDT efficiency highly depends on the oxygen content presented in tumor tissues, and the anaerobic environment in solid tumors has greatly limited the PDT therapeutic effect. To tackle these dilemmas, researchers have proposed the development of multifunctional therapeutic systems (MFTSs) that combine phototherapy (i.e., PTT, PDT) and chemotherapy to enhance the therapeutic efficiency. Indeed, studies shown that with MFTSs, the therapeutic efficiencies were warranted and disadvantages associated with the isolated therapeutic methods could be well minimized [235–239]. For instance, Zhang et al. constructed a C-dots based MFTS capable of simultaneous targeted drug delivery, PTT and PDT [238]; in vivo experiments demonstrated that tumors of nude mice could be completely eradicated after injected with the MFTS and exerted laser irradiation, highlighting the effectiveness in tumor removal by the chemical-light synergistic treatment (Fig. 13F). Similarly, Do et al. prepared a cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) based MFTS by combining (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), DOX, folic acid and C-dots for simultaneous imaging, drug delivery and PTT/PDT treatments in which DOX achieved pH and heat dual-response-controlled release, significantly reducing the side effect while still maintaining the anticancer activity against cervical cancer cells [239].

Metal ion sensing: Metal ions are among the most common detection targets of red/NIR C-dots in biosensing. C-dots based sensing probes have successfully applied for the detection and tracking of majority metal ions including Cr3+ [240], Al3+ [241], Fe3+ [242], Fe2+ [243], Pb2+ [244], Mg2+ [245], Ag+ [246] and etc. In these reports, sensing of metal ions was mainly accomplished via photo-induced electron transfer (PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced charge transfer (PCT) and inner filtering effect (IFE) [247–250]. Although noble metals (i.e., Au, Pt) are commonly used for jewelry in daily life, their potential biotoxicity has raised concerns. As such, Gao et al. developed a red-emissive C-dots based sensing system for the effective detection of noble metal ions Pt2+, Au3+ and Pd2+, in which the PL of C-dots could be quenched in the presence of these noble metal ions and the degree of PL quenching was linearly related to the concentrations of metal ions (Fig. 14 A). Furthermore, the study also shown that the C-dots could be applied as an intracellular and in vivo fluorescent probe for noble metal ions detection [251]. Iron is an indispensable trace element in human body and present in hemoglobin in the form of Fe3+; the proper sensing of Fe3+ is also of great importance. In 2019, Bao et al. built a dual-functional sensing platform for the highly sensitive, real-time detection of Fe3+ and l-cysteine via an “on-off-on” mechanism [252]. In a similar study, Tang and co-workers also accomplished the highly sensitive and selective, sequential detection of Fe3+ and l-ascorbic acid with a NIR C-dots based sensing probe [113].

pH sensing: Acid-base equilibrium of body is closely related to human health. The body fluids pH of healthy individuals is maintained between 7.35 and 7.45 and an overly acidic constitution can easily cause various diseases. Hence, the detection and monitoring of pH environment in human body is particularly significant. C-dots based biological pH sensors are functioning based on the interaction of protons with the surface functionalities of C-dots, thus they could also be regarded as a special type of ion sensor. In 2019, Ye et al. reported the synthesis of red-emissive C-dots with two-photon fluorescent emissions for the intracellular pH sensing [253]. The C-dots exhibited a linear pH-sensitive response in the range of 3.5–6.5, which was ideal for pH tracking in living cells. Most importantly, the as-prepared C-dots could be successfully used to sense and visualize the pH fluctuations of mice tissues and zebrafish, highlighting their great potential in the monitoring of live biological systems. In a different study, Ci et al. synthesized NIR-II C-dots, the PL of which could be quenched due to severe particle aggregation as the pH increased [254]. Exploiting this phenomenon, the authors successfully realized the in vivo, non-invasive monitoring of gastric pH changes during the digestion process in mice (Fig. 14B), highlighting the great potential of the NIR-II C-dots as probe for the real time monitoring of pH values in organisms.

Biomolecule sensing: Biomolecules are chemical substances that either make up the living organisms or play important roles to the human health. By carefully monitoring biomolecules in human body, researchers could understand the mechanism of disease occurrence more accurately and provide better methods for disease treatment [255]. As such, red/NIR C-dots have been heavily applied as platforms for the development of small biomolecules sensing systems, achieving the sensitive and selective detection of peptide [29], adrenaline [119], amino acid [256,257], glucose [258], phosphate anions [259] and vitamins [260,261]. For instance, Feng et al. successfully realized the “off-on” sensing of vitamin C with NIR C-dots composites, in which quenched C-dots PL emissions could be readily restored in the presence of vitamin C (Fig. 14C) [261]. Similarly, Yang et al. realized the “off-on” sensing of cortisol by disrupting the inner filtering effect that quenched the C-dots PL emissions in the presence of cortisol [119].

In addition to small biomolecules, biomacromolecules possess more complex structures and serve even more important functions in living organisms, thus red/NIR C-dots based probes for the sensing of biomacromolecules including proteins [262,263], RNA [264,265] and antigen [266] have also been frequently reported. For instance, Yin et al. developed a wash-free fluorescent sensor for the detection of RNA with red-emissive C-dots (Fig. 14D) [265]. The positive benzothiazolium groups of C-dots could respond with negatively charged RNA via electrostatic attraction, achieving a detection limit of 0.62 μg/mL. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) act as broad-spectrum tumor markers, thus have important clinical significance for the early diagnosis of malignant tumors. To properly detect CEA, Shao et al. designed a fluorescent aptamer sensor exploiting the FRET transfer between NIR C-dots and gold nanorods, which achieved a linear range of 0.1–5000 pg/mL, and a significantly low detection limit of 0.02 pg/mL [266].

Herein, the rapid advancements of red/NIR C-dots in the biomedical fields have been comprehensively summarized and discussed. We firstly discussed the mainstream syntheses methods for the preparation of red/NIR C-dots briefly, which were mainly realized via bottom-up approaches. Next, carbon precursors that are frequently used for the preparation of red/NIR C-dots including citric acid, amines, phenols, polymers, cyanine dyes, biomass, biomolecules as well as inorganic chemicals have been systematically covered and disseminated. Then, the four mainstream mechanisms including core-state, surface-state, molecular-state and CEE emissions for the explanation of red/NIR C-dots fluorescence were briefly discussed. On the basis of the PL mechanism, three strategies for the tuning of red/NIR C-dots fluorescence emissions including heteroatom doping, reaction parameters tuning and C-dots structure design were carefully detailed. Lastly, recent developments of red/NIR C-dots for bioimaging (i.e., fluorescent, photoacoustic and multi-modal imaging), drug delivery, photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, multifunctional therapy, and biosensing (i.e., metal ion, pH and biomolecule sensing) were also carefully presented.

Despite the above-discussed advantages and the increasingly growing applications, the clinical and commercial applications of red/NIR C-dots are still in its infancy, and much challenge still needs to be tackled to fulfill the true potential of red/NIR C-dots in the biomedical fields. Firstly, the synthesis of red/NIR C-dots should be streamlined and standardized. Currently, the synthesis methods for the preparation of red/NIR C-dots are dominated by bottom-up approaches. Although “top-down” method could obtain C-dots with controllable size, some deficiencies such as limited carbon precursors, low yields and non-red-dominated emissions restricted the wide adoption of this approach for the preparation of red/NIR C-dots. As for “bottom-up” methods, the wide availability of proper carbon precursors and less stringent reaction conditions make it a versatile strategy for red/NIR C-dots preparation. Furthermore, the reaction parameters (i.e., temperature, pressure, etc.) could be facilely manipulated, making it very handy to study the PL mechanism and tune the emissions of red/NIR C-dots. However, many of these “bottom-up” synthesis methods are insufficient to obtain pure C-dots and their products are likely composed by some limited nanoscale carbon domains and molecular chromophores survived from carbonization process [267]. In extreme cases, red/NIR C-dots reported by different research groups exhibited vastly different properties even though they were synthesized from identical carbon precursors and synthetic methods, significantly discouraging their clinical and commercial applications. As such, significant attention should be devoted to streamline the syntheses of red/NIR C-dots with scalability, reproducibility and standability [268,269].

Secondly, more efforts should be focused on the investigation and evaluation of the toxicity and biocompatibility of red/NIR C-dots to pave the way for their eventual clinical applications. As demonstrated by numerous studies, C-dots are inherently less toxic than traditional materials [270–274]. Still, toxicities of C-dots have been disclosed in various studies [275,276]. To expedite the clinical and commercial applications of red/NIR C-dots, their biocompatibility, toxicity and potential biodegradation pathways should be extensively investigated. Unfortunately, this has been less studied or not even seriously touched. Toxicity and biocompatibility of C-dots could be heavily influenced by their synthesis conditions, concentrations and doses applied. As a result, vastly different, or even contradictive conclusions could be drawn if they were not correctly and sufficiently evaluated [277–280]. Thus, even though it is challenging, more efforts should be focused on the proper and sufficient evaluation of the toxicity and biocompatibility of red/NIR C-dots.

Thirdly, to further enhance the property and expand the application scopes of red/NIR C-dots, more efforts should be devoted to the preparation of their solid-state emissive (SSE) counterparts. Unlike the regular, liquid-dispersed-state emitting only red/NIR C-dots, SSE C-dots not only exhibited higher PL efficiency [281,282], but also demonstrated convenient properties tuning. Specifically, SSE C-dots could obtain phosphorescence that was hardly achieved in regular red/NIR C-dots by dispersing in stable and rigid matrices [283,284]; similarly, the biocompatibility of SSE C-dots could be greatly enhanced by dispersing them in matrices of superior biocompatibility [285,286].

Last but not least, more attention should be paid to the development of C-dots with high PLQYs and emitting in the NIR-II (900–1700 nm) region. Compared to C-dots emitting in the red to NIR-I (600–900 nm) regions, NIR-II C-dots that could achieve much deeper tissue penetration, significantly minimized spontaneous fluorescence interference and improved bioimaging resolutions were rarely reported [112,227,254]. The reason that NIR-II C-dots were less reported could be attributed to the fact that the energy bandgaps of C-dots are mainly located in the UV–vis region and extremely hard to be tuned to the NIR-II region. To conquer this challenge, strategies including surface modifications by electron-accepting [40,227] or -donating groups [287], size control of the π-π conjugating domains [254] and coupling with NIR-II dye derivatives [288] have been attempted to set up new luminescent band gaps in the NIR-II region. In addition, the fact that C-dots could inherit some characteristics of their carbon precursors provide new perspectives for the synthesis of NIR-II C-dots using precursors with intrinsic NIR- II luminescence.
